name: Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Natural Trail Loop
name_original: Aliseda desde Malpartida de Cáceres — circular por Cáceres y la Sierra de San Pedro
description: Cycle 56.6 miles through Cáceres and the Sierra de San Pedro, enjoying diverse landscapes and historical sights on this moderate route.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Malpartida De Cáceres traverse a landscape characterized by open plains, rolling hills, and several significant reservoirs. The region offers varied terrain, from routes following natural corridors to those circling large bodies of water. Elevation gains are generally moderate, with some longer routes featuring more sustained climbs. Best road cycling routes around Malpartida De Cáceres The most popular road cycling route is Pantano de Valdesalor — circular desde Cáceres, a 31.1 miles (50.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 20 minutes to complete. This moderate circular route offers views of the Valdesalor Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Natural Trail Loop, a moderate 56.6 miles (91.0 km) path. This route follows a natural corridor, providing a mix of open and varied scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Cáceres - Trujillo Loop via Valdesalor Reservoir, a 75.8 miles (122.0 km) trail leading through open countryside and past the Valdesalor Reservoir, often completed in about 5 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Malpartida De Cáceres is defined by open plains, reservoir views, and natural corridors. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various skill levels. The routes in Malpartida De Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Malpartida De Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Malpartida De Cáceres?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes in the Malpartida De Cáceres area. These routes cater to various skill levels, with 9 easy, 19 moderate, and 9 difficult options available.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Malpartida De Cáceres?
A: Yes, the region offers 9 easy road cycling routes perfect for beginners. These routes typically feature gentler terrain and shorter distances, allowing for a relaxed introduction to road cycling in the area.

Q: Where can I find more challenging road cycling routes near Malpartida De Cáceres?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 9 difficult road cycling routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058679122/caceres-trujillo-loop-via-valdesalor-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres - Trujillo Loop via Valdesalor Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 75.8 miles (122.0 km) and includes significant elevation gains.

Q: Are there circular road bike routes in the Malpartida De Cáceres area?
A: Many routes in the area are circular, offering convenient start and end points. A popular circular route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190771720/pantano-de-valdesalor-circular-desde-caceres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pantano de Valdesalor — circular desde Cáceres&lt;/a&gt;, which provides scenic views of the Valdesalor Reservoir.

Q: What are some road cycling routes around 50km in length?
A: If you're looking for a route around 50km, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190771720/pantano-de-valdesalor-circular-desde-caceres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pantano de Valdesalor — circular desde Cáceres&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. It's 31.1 miles (50.1 km) long and typically takes about 2 hours 20 minutes to complete.

Q: What natural sights or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes often pass by significant natural and historical sites. You can explore areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;, known for its unique rock formations, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4396799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lower Barrueco Pond&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also offer glimpses of the Valdesalor Reservoir.

Q: Are there any historical sites or attractions near the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. You might find yourself cycling near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4755551" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Centre of Cáceres&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, or passing by structures like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5674973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre de Bujaco&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6222082" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palace and Tower of the Storks&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Malpartida De Cáceres?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from open plains to reservoir views, and the well-maintained routes that cater to different skill levels.

Q: Are there any longer road cycling routes for endurance training?
A: For longer rides, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057886509/caceres-badajoz-corridor-natural-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres-Badajoz Corridor Natural Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 56.6 miles (91.0 km) and follows a natural corridor, offering a mix of open and varied scenery for extended cycling.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Malpartida De Cáceres?
A: The terrain around Malpartida De Cáceres is characterized by open plains, rolling hills, and routes that often follow natural corridors or circle significant reservoirs. Elevation gains are generally moderate, though some longer routes include more sustained climbs.

Q: Can I find routes that include reservoir views?
A: Absolutely. Several routes offer stunning views of the region's reservoirs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31918234" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse Molano loop from Cáceres&lt;/a&gt; is one such route that provides scenic vistas of the reservoir.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by the iconic Osborne Bull?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6910553" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse Molano – Osborne Bull on the N-630 loop from Arroyo de la Luz&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that will take you past the famous Osborne Bull landmark, offering a unique photo opportunity during your ride.
tips: Leave the N-521 national road by a small detour to see two old bridges over the Salor river. If you have time, take a look at the Marmitas, small river pillars curved into the rocky river bed.

There have been several archaeological finds that indicate an origin of Aliseda much earlier than the 13th century. The actual date of the city's founding is unknown, but the area has been used in some form since before Christ.

A defensive castle from the 14th century was built to protect Cáceres and the trade routes to and from Badajoz. Today, the palace is the private residence of the Viscount of Torre Hidalgo and the lush gardens are rented out for events.

It is presided over by the Town Hall built in 1869 by Ignacio María de Michelena. In it you can see such emblematic monuments as the Torre de la Hierba, the Torre de los Púlpitos and especially the Torre de Bujaco next to the Hermitage of Peace.

The peculiar structure of this bullring testifies to its historical past, which has earned it being declared a Site of Cultural Interest.
The Cáceres Bullring, also known as “Coso de la Era de los Mártires”, is one of the oldest in Spain.
Its construction began in 1844 and the works were completed two years later. The first bullfight was held on August 6, 1846, with bulls led by D. Gaspar Muñoz.
The square is built with granite and masonry walls, it has a capacity of 8,000 spectators spread over three floors.
In addition to bullfighting celebrations, it is also used for other types of events such as music concerts.

Malpartida de Cáceres is a small town along the national highway N-521 west of Cáceres. The town is the gateway to Los Barruecos Natural Monument, a series of lakes surrounded by a landscape of granite rocks.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is named "Aliseda from Malpartida de Cáceres", suggesting Malpartida de Cáceres as a primary starting and parking point. You can find suitable parking within the town before beginning your circular ride.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: This is primarily a road cycling route, so you'll find generally well-paved surfaces. However, the Cáceres region can have some unpaved segments, so be prepared for varied conditions. The route traverses river valleys and areas with mountainous features, especially through the Sierra de San Pedro, which features rocky sections and dehesa landscapes.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the beautiful and diverse scenery of Extremadura. Expect Mediterranean forests, striking granite rock formations, and expansive plains. The Sierra de San Pedro section is particularly picturesque, with forests of chestnut, oak, and holm-oak trees, and the unique dehesa agroforestry system.

faqs.question: What notable sights will I encounter on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route offers several interesting points. You'll pass through the settlement of Aliseda and the starting point of Malpartida de Cáceres. Other highlights include the Marmites of the Salor River, the historic Palacio de las Seguras, and the iconic Cáceres Main Square. You might also see the Era de los Mártires Bullring.

faqs.question: What wildlife can I see along the route, especially in the Sierra de San Pedro?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de San Pedro is a Special Protection Area for Birds (ZEPA), making it an excellent spot for birdwatching. You might spot species like the Iberian imperial eagle, black vulture, and black stork. The dehesa landscapes also support diverse flora and fauna.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: To enjoy the route comfortably, consider cycling during spring or autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for a 91 km ride with moderate elevation changes. Summers in Extremadura can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is on public roads and through natural areas, it's a long road cycling route. Bringing dogs on such a long ride, especially with potential traffic and the distance involved, might not be ideal for their safety and comfort. If you do bring a dog, ensure they are well-trained, can keep up, and are always on a leash when appropriate.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It largely follows public roads and paths through natural areas that are freely accessible.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of other paths. Notably, it passes through parts of the Camino Natural del Corredor Cáceres - Badajoz and the Camino Natural Corredor Cáceres-Badajoz. Ruta Cáceres-Badajoz-Embalse de Piedra Aguda. You'll also find smaller segments of trails like the Cañada Real del Puerto del Pico and the Cordel de La Enjarada y La Segura.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: As the route passes through settlements like Aliseda and Malpartida de Cáceres, you will find opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel or grab a drink. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer stretches between towns.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate and requires good fitness due to its 91 km distance and 700 meters of elevation gain. While the surfaces are generally good, it's not typically recommended for absolute beginners. Cyclists with some experience and a decent fitness level will find it more enjoyable.
